title: 1.3.0 - Pens & Paper | Drawing Machines 101

full_text: Well, hey and welcome to the pens and paper for pen plotting video. And that was actually slightly harder to say than I was suspecting. This was going to be two videos, one about pens, one about paper, but I decided it makes more sense to bundle them together because of the way pens and paper interact. It's actually kind of important. And between you and me, I suspect over the long term, this video is going to get the most views out of all of these videos. Because the question I get asked the most is, "Hey Dan, which pen should I use for pen plotting? What's the best pen?" And everyone's searching for the perfect pen. There isn't one single one, but I think there are some pens that are a lot better than others. And once again, against the YouTube video editors and creators secret policy where they say, "Put the answer right at the end of the video." I'm going to tell you up front right now the six pens and the three papers to get might only be five pens. We'll find out. So here are the answers and then I'll explain how we got there and why they may or may not be right and then all the nuances and then in the end we'll have some special cases. Let's do that. So onwards these are the pens that you should get. Quick heads up before we start. I am really bad at pronouncing things. I will get manufacturers names wrong. I will get pen names wrong. I'll pronounce them all wrong. Don't worry. There should be a link down below to penplotter.com. There's a resources section on there. There's links to all of these pens. So, everything I say here, you will find down there spelled correctly, maybe not pronounced correctly. The first pen you're probably going to get is a Secura Pigma Micron pen. It's a technical pen. It's got super fine lines. They're really great. Everybody loves these pens. We're going to make this quick. Let's go on to the next one. Second, the Stabilo. Stabilo. Stabilo. Okay, enough of that. The Stablio.88 colorful pens. You're probably going to move on to these. Get some color in there. Highly recommend these. These are great. Lots of people use them. The hexagonal shape makes it slightly annoying to align the pens up when you change colors, but we'll get on to that in a couple of videos time. So, that's pen number two. Pen number three, actually, this is a two for one. After exploring all those pens, people go, "Hey, white ink is cool." We'll get on to that in a tick. So, these are the Secura again jelly rolls. These are the jelly roll white. They come in all sorts of other colors as well and metallics and things like that. People swear by these. I also use these ones here which are the Uniball UM 120 NM. I think like that. Again, the links are down below. I use metallics instead of white, although they do have white. And the reason for that is I think these are temperature dependent and I'm in a very cold studio. Some people say they have no problems with these ones. I think these need to be in a warmer environment. I have better luck with these ones. Some people say these are terrible and these are great. Anyway, that's by the by white pens. Either the Uniball or the Jelly Roll for that. Okay, swiftly on to number four. This is a little bit of an odd choice, but this is the Uniball eye pen. I use the fine and I use the Micro. I really like these because you can see the amount of ink that is left in there, which is super useful. We'll come on to that again in a moment.
